network.overall_strategy: The network integrates four literature-validated prebiotic routes to valine: (1) Strecker synthesis from isobutyraldehyde (Miller & Schlesinger, 1993), (2) cyanosulfidic photochemistry building the branched carbon skeleton from HCN and small sugars (Patel et al., 2015), (3) reductive amination of α-ketoisovalerate on Ni minerals (Kaur et al., 2024), and (4) spark-discharge/atmospheric chemistry (Johnson et al., 2009). Hydrothermal CO₂ fixation provides formate, formaldehyde, and pyruvate as upstream feedstocks. Pyruvate serves as a central metabolic hub, feeding both the keto-acid reductive amination pathway (via α-ketoisovalerate) and the cyanosulfidic pathway (via decarboxylation to acetone). Isobutyraldehyde is a key convergence point reached independently by cyanosulfidic photoreduction and Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis. Valinonitrile (α-aminonitrile of valine) is the penultimate intermediate shared by both Strecker and cyanosulfidic routes. Multiple environmental interconnections link deep-sea hydrothermal production of C1-C3 building blocks to surface UV-driven and cyanosulfidic chemistry, culminating in biochemical assembly steps.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent, pH 9-11, H2-rich fluids from serpentinization

reactions.mechanism: CO2 is adsorbed on the Fe3S4 surface which acts as an analog of biological [Fe4S4] clusters. H2 dissociates on the mineral surface and reduces CO2 via surface-bound formate intermediate. The FeS/Fe3S4 surface provides electrons for the two-electron reduction.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction to formate is well-established in hydrothermal origin-of-life chemistry. This provides the foundational C1 building block that feeds downstream formaldehyde and higher-carbon synthesis. Serpentinization-generated H2 is the reductant in alkaline vent settings.

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Formate is further reduced on magnetite surfaces via dissociative adsorption of H2. The reaction proceeds through a surface-bound formyl intermediate that is released as formaldehyde. Magnetite provides the catalytic surface for Fischer-Tropsch-type stepwise reduction.
reactions.reasoning: Magnetite-catalyzed reduction of formate to formaldehyde is part of the Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) synthesis observed in hydrothermal systems. Formaldehyde is a critical C1 building block for formose-type sugar synthesis. This step connects hydrothermal CO2 fixation to surface sugar chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation to pyruvate

conditions.other: alkaline vent, sustained H2 flux, possibly via formate and acetate intermediates

reactions.mechanism: Sequential CO2 reduction on FeS/Fe3O4 surfaces proceeds through formate → acetate → pyruvate via surface-catalyzed carbonylation and reduction steps. This mimics the reductive acetyl-CoA pathway. The Fe-Ni-S mineral surface facilitates C-C bond formation.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate synthesis from CO2 and H2 on iron-sulfur mineral surfaces has been demonstrated in hydrothermal analog experiments. Pyruvate is a central metabolic hub in all known biology and serves as a critical branching point in this network, feeding both the keto-acid reductive amination route (via α-ketoisovalerate) and the decarboxylation route to acetone.

reactions.name: Formose reaction: formaldehyde to glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: alkaline evaporitic pool, Ca(OH)2 or borate catalyst, warm pond conditions

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde undergoes aldol condensation in the presence of base (Ca(OH)2). Glycolaldehyde forms first (2 HCHO → HOCH2CHO), then a further aldol with formaldehyde gives glyceraldehyde. Borate minerals complex with the cis-diols of sugars, stabilizing glyceraldehyde against degradation and channeling the formose reaction toward C3 products.
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction is a well-established prebiotic route to sugars from formaldehyde. Borate stabilization of sugars (particularly ribose and glyceraldehyde) was demonstrated by Ricardo et al. (2004). Glyceraldehyde is explicitly identified by Patel et al. (2015) as a key precursor in the cyanosulfidic pathway to valine, where it serves as the carbon source for acetone formation.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoreduction of glyceraldehyde to acetone

conditions.other: UV irradiation (~254 nm), shallow sulfidic pond, phosphate buffer

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde first isomerizes to dihydroxyacetone under mildly basic conditions. UV-driven Cu(I)/Cu(II) redox cycling on sulfide mineral surfaces facilitates stepwise deoxygenation of dihydroxyacetone, with H2S serving as the terminal reductant. The net effect is photoreductive loss of oxygen from dihydroxyacetone to yield acetone.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry) demonstrated that glyceraldehyde is converted to acetone via dihydroxyacetone under UV irradiation with H2S as reductant in the presence of Cu(I)/Cu(II) redox cycling. This is a key step in the cyanosulfidic protometabolism that connects sugar chemistry to amino acid precursors. Cu2S minerals provide the photosensitizer (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetone

conditions.catalyst: metal ions (Mn2+, Fe2+) on clay surfaces
conditions.other: geothermal pool, mildly acidic pH, divalent metal cations catalyze decarboxylation

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes β-decarboxylation at elevated temperatures. The keto group facilitates loss of CO2 from the carboxyl group. On mineral surfaces with divalent cations (Mn2+, Fe2+), the decarboxylation is accelerated. This produces acetaldehyde from pyruvate directly, but under conditions where two pyruvates undergo an acetolactate-like condensation followed by decarboxylation, acetone can be produced.
reactions.reasoning: Thermal decarboxylation of α-keto acids is well known in prebiotic chemistry. This reaction provides an alternative route to acetone from hydrothermal pyruvate, connecting the hydrothermal CO2-fixation pathway to the surface cyanosulfidic chemistry. While direct pyruvate → acetone is somewhat speculative (pyruvate → acetaldehyde is more standard), condensation of two pyruvates to acetolactate followed by decarboxylation to acetone is biochemically analogous to the valine biosynthetic pathway. Clay minerals and metal ions facilitate the process.

reactions.name: Cyanohydrin formation: acetone + HCN

conditions.other: weakly basic pH ~8-9, concentrated HCN in evaporitic pool

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of cyanide ion (CN⁻) to the carbonyl carbon of acetone. The reaction is reversible but favored at moderate concentrations. The resulting acetone cyanohydrin (2-hydroxy-2-methylpropanenitrile, compound 23 in Patel et al.) has the branched C4 skeleton needed for valine.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) explicitly describe the formation of acetone cyanohydrin (compound 23) as the first step in the cyanosulfidic homologation sequence toward valine. This is a classic organic reaction that proceeds spontaneously in aqueous solution when HCN/CN⁻ is present. Montmorillonite may concentrate reactants in drying pools.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic thioamide formation from cyanohydrin

conditions.other: dark reaction (no UV needed), sulfidic aqueous solution, pH ~7-9

reactions.mechanism: Hydrosulfide (HS⁻) performs nucleophilic addition to the nitrile group of acetone cyanohydrin, converting the C≡N to a thioamide (C(=S)NH2). The resulting α-hydroxythioamide (compound 24 in Patel et al.) retains the hydroxyl from the cyanohydrin and the branched carbon skeleton.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) detail this dark-phase step where HS⁻ converts the cyanohydrin nitrile to a thioamide. This is compound 24 in their scheme (Fig. 1b). The reaction proceeds under mild aqueous conditions with dissolved sulfide, consistent with sulfidic surface waters. Cu2S and other sulfide minerals provide a sustained source of HS⁻.

reactions.name: Photoreductive desulfurization to isobutyraldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation (~254 nm), H2S as terminal reductant, shallow sunlit pond

reactions.mechanism: UV irradiation drives Cu(I)/Cu(II) cycling on sulfide mineral surfaces. The α-hydroxythioamide (24) undergoes stepwise photoreduction: first loss of the thioamide sulfur to give thioamide (25), then further UV-driven reduction with H2S as electron donor cleaves the C-S bond and reduces the thioamide to an aldehyde. The product is isobutyraldehyde (2-methylpropanal), preserving the branched C4 skeleton.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) demonstrated the UV-driven conversion of hydroxythioamide intermediates to aldehydes via sequential photoreduction with H2S. Compounds 24→25→aldehyde in their Fig. 1b scheme. The aldehyde produced from the acetone-derived thioamide is isobutyraldehyde, which is explicitly identified by Miller & Schlesinger (1993) as 'the Strecker precursor to valine.' This step is the critical photochemical transformation linking cyanosulfidic chemistry to Strecker synthesis.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis: isobutyraldehyde to valinonitrile

conditions.catalyst: Zn2+ or Fe2+ ions on clay surfaces
conditions.other: pH 8-10 (free NH3 required, pKaH~9.2), concentrated evaporitic pool; alternatively DAP (diamidophosphate) enables reaction at neutral pH

reactions.mechanism: Classical Strecker synthesis: (1) NH3 attacks the aldehyde carbonyl of isobutyraldehyde to form a carbinolamine, (2) dehydration gives an imine (Schiff base), (3) CN⁻ adds to the electrophilic imine carbon to yield the α-aminonitrile (valinonitrile, compound 27 in Patel et al.). Divalent metal ions (Zn2+, Fe2+) stabilize the imine intermediate and accelerate step 2.
reactions.reasoning: Miller & Schlesinger (1993) explicitly identified isobutyraldehyde as 'the Strecker precursor to valine.' The Strecker synthesis is the canonical prebiotic route to amino acids from aldehydes. Patel et al. (2015) showed that NH3 addition to the cyanohydrin of isobutyraldehyde (compound 26) gives valinonitrile (compound 27) in ~42% yield. Powner et al. (2019, Commun. Chem.) demonstrated that DAP promotes Strecker aminonitrile formation at neutral pH with 73-quantitative% yields. Metal cations on montmorillonite catalyze the imine formation step (Miller & Schlesinger, 1993).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of valinonitrile to valine

conditions.other: mildly acidic or basic aqueous solution, prolonged heating accelerates hydrolysis; geothermal pool

reactions.mechanism: Two-step hydrolysis of the nitrile group: (1) water attacks the nitrile carbon to form an amide intermediate (valinamide), (2) further hydrolysis of the amide gives the carboxylic acid (valine) with release of NH3. The overall transformation is C≡N + 2H2O → COOH + NH3.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to amino acids is the final step in the Strecker synthesis and is well-established in prebiotic chemistry. Both acid-catalyzed and thermal hydrolysis pathways have been demonstrated. This step converts valinonitrile (the penultimate intermediate common to both Strecker and cyanosulfidic routes) into the target amino acid valine. The reaction is thermodynamically favorable in aqueous solution.

reactions.name: Pyruvate homologation to alpha-ketoisovalerate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, mineral-confined micropores, aldol/decarboxylation cascade

reactions.mechanism: Two pyruvate molecules undergo an acetolactate-like condensation on FeS mineral surfaces (analogous to the biological acetolactate synthase step in valine biosynthesis). The resulting acetolactate intermediate undergoes reductive isomerization and decarboxylation to yield α-ketoisovalerate. Alternatively, pyruvate condenses with formaldehyde/acetaldehyde via aldol chemistry to build the branched C5 skeleton, followed by oxidation to the keto acid.
reactions.reasoning: α-Ketoisovalerate is the biological precursor to valine via transamination, and is identified by Kaur et al. (2024) as the direct substrate for reductive amination to valine. Its prebiotic synthesis likely mirrors the biological pathway: condensation of pyruvate units. While the exact prebiotic mechanism is not fully established (this is an acknowledged open question in the literature), mineral-catalyzed aldol chemistry on FeS surfaces provides a plausible route. The branched carbon skeleton formation from C2-C3 keto acid units is thermodynamically accessible under hydrothermal conditions.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of alpha-ketoisovalerate to valine

conditions.catalyst: Ni nanoparticles or Ni-Fe meteorite powder
conditions.other: 150 mM NH4HCO3, pH 7-8, 72 h reaction time; also viable with FeNi alloy meteorite catalysts

reactions.mechanism: H2 dissociates on Ni metal surfaces, providing surface-bound hydride. The α-keto group of α-ketoisovalerate is reduced by surface hydride while NH3/NH4+ provides the amino group via reductive amination. The mechanism involves: (1) imine formation between the keto group and NH3, (2) hydrogenation of the imine C=N bond by surface-bound H atoms. Competing reduction to α-hydroxy acid occurs but amino acid is the major product.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur et al. (2024, Chem) demonstrated that Ni nanoparticles and Ni-rich meteorite powder catalyze H2-driven reductive amination of α-ketoisovalerate to valine at room temperature and pH 8. With Ni catalysts, yields of 10-50% valine were achieved; Pd catalysts gave up to 80% yield with >5:1 selectivity for amino acid over hydroxy acid. No reaction occurred without metal catalyst. Controls confirmed the requirement for both H2 and metal catalyst. This 'metabolism-like' hydrogenation is the most direct prebiotic route to valine from its keto acid precursor.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of isobutyraldehyde

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch-type conditions, CO from volcanic/hydrothermal outgassing, high H2 partial pressure

reactions.mechanism: CO and H2 undergo stepwise chain growth on magnetite surfaces via the Fischer-Tropsch mechanism: CO adsorbs and dissociates on Fe sites, surface carbide/methylidyne species are hydrogenated, and C-C coupling builds chains. Branching can occur via secondary insertion pathways. Isobutyraldehyde is a minor product of FTT synthesis alongside linear aldehydes and alcohols.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on magnetite and iron surfaces produces a distribution of hydrocarbons, alcohols, and aldehydes including branched products. While linear products dominate, branched C4 aldehydes like isobutyraldehyde are plausible minor products. This provides an alternative hydrothermal source of the key Strecker precursor to valine, bypassing the multi-step cyanosulfidic route. The yield of branched products is low, which is consistent with the noted challenge of sourcing branched precursors prebiotically.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine

conditions.catalyst: FeS/Fe3S4 mineral surfaces, or Ni minerals
conditions.other: alkaline vent, NH3 from serpentinization, H2 abundant

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate reacts with NH3 to form an imine (pyruvate-NH3 Schiff base), which is then reduced by H2 on the mineral surface to yield alanine. This is analogous to the reductive amination of α-ketoisovalerate (rxn_013) but with the simpler C3 keto acid substrate.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine on mineral surfaces is well-established in prebiotic chemistry. Kaur et al. (2024) demonstrated analogous H2-driven reductive amination of multiple α-keto acids on Ni catalysts. Alanine is needed as an amino group donor for the transamination route to valine (rxn_016). This reaction also demonstrates that the reductive amination mechanism works for both C3 and C5 keto acids.

reactions.name: Transamination: alpha-ketoisovalerate + alanine to valine + pyruvate

conditions.catalyst: pyridoxal (vitamin B6 analog) or metal ions (Fe2+, Cu2+)
conditions.other: prebiotic pool with mixed amino and keto acids, pH 5-8; metal-ion catalysis demonstrated

reactions.mechanism: Transamination proceeds via a pyridoxal-phosphate-like mechanism: the amino group of alanine is transferred to the keto group of α-ketoisovalerate. (1) Alanine forms a Schiff base with a carbonyl catalyst (pyridoxal analog or metal complex), (2) tautomerization shifts the imine, (3) hydrolysis releases pyruvate and the amino group is transferred to α-ketoisovalerate yielding valine. Metal ions (Cu2+, Fe2+) can catalyze this without pyridoxal.
reactions.reasoning: Transamination is a fundamental metabolic reaction. Miller & Schlesinger (1993) noted that α-ketoisovalerate participates in transamination networks in prebiotic chemistry. Metal-catalyzed transamination between amino acids and keto acids has been demonstrated under mild conditions. This reaction creates a catalytic cycle: pyruvate is regenerated and can re-enter the network (rxn_012 or rxn_015), making the synthesis partially autocatalytic.

pathways.name: Full cyanosulfidic pathway from CO2
pathways.description: Complete route from CO2 through hydrothermal C1 fixation, surface formose chemistry, and Patel et al. cyanosulfidic photochemistry to valine via valinonitrile. CO2 is reduced to formate then formaldehyde in hydrothermal vents; formaldehyde enters surface formose reaction to yield glyceraldehyde; UV-driven cyanosulfidic photoreduction converts glyceraldehyde to acetone; multi-step HCN/H2S chemistry builds the branched C4 aldehyde; Strecker addition gives valinonitrile; hydrolysis yields valine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate decarboxylation to cyanosulfidic pathway
pathways.description: Pyruvate formed by hydrothermal CO2 fixation undergoes thermal decarboxylation to acetone at the surface, then enters the cyanosulfidic homologation sequence. This connects deep-sea carbon fixation directly to the Patel et al. photochemistry without requiring the formose reaction.

pathways.name: Direct hydrothermal reductive amination
pathways.description: The most direct route: CO2 is fixed to pyruvate in hydrothermal vents, pyruvate is homologated to α-ketoisovalerate, and H2-driven reductive amination on Ni minerals yields valine. This entirely hydrothermal pathway follows the Kaur et al. (2024) metabolism-like mechanism.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch to Strecker pathway
pathways.description: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on magnetite produces isobutyraldehyde directly from CO and H2 in hydrothermal vents. This aldehyde is then transported to surface environments where classical Strecker synthesis (with NH3 and HCN) gives valinonitrile, which hydrolyzes to valine.

pathways.name: Transamination route via alanine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal synthesis provides both α-ketoisovalerate (from pyruvate homologation) and alanine (from pyruvate reductive amination). These combine in a biochemical transamination reaction, transferring the amino group from alanine to α-ketoisovalerate to yield valine and regenerate pyruvate. This creates a proto-metabolic catalytic cycle.

pathways.name: Surface-only cyanosulfidic from formaldehyde
pathways.description: Starting from exogenous formaldehyde (e.g., from atmospheric photochemistry or interplanetary delivery), the entire synthesis occurs at the surface: formose reaction to glyceraldehyde, photoreduction to acetone, cyanosulfidic homologation to isobutyraldehyde, Strecker to valinonitrile, hydrolysis to valine.

pathways.name: Convergent acetone pathway (hydrothermal + surface)
pathways.description: Acetone is produced by two independent routes — cyanosulfidic photoreduction of glyceraldehyde (rxn_005) and thermal decarboxylation of hydrothermal pyruvate (rxn_006) — and then feeds into the single cyanosulfidic homologation sequence to valine. This illustrates convergent synthesis at the acetone hub.

pathways.name: Convergent isobutyraldehyde pathway
pathways.description: Isobutyraldehyde is produced by two independent routes — cyanosulfidic photoreduction of α-hydroxythioamide (rxn_009) and Fischer-Tropsch synthesis (rxn_014) — and then enters the Strecker synthesis. This demonstrates convergent supply of the key branched C4 aldehyde.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal keto-acid to surface Strecker hybrid
pathways.description: α-Ketoisovalerate formed hydrothermally (rxn_003 → rxn_012) is not reductively aminated but instead undergoes decarboxylation or reduction at the surface to yield isobutyraldehyde, which then enters the Strecker pathway. This hybrid route uses hydrothermal keto-acid production but surface Strecker chemistry for the final amino acid formation.

pathways.name: Autocatalytic transamination cycle from cyanosulfidic valine
pathways.description: Valine produced via the cyanosulfidic/Strecker pathway (P1 or P2) can itself serve as an amino donor in transamination with α-ketoisovalerate, amplifying valine production. Meanwhile, pyruvate regenerated in the transamination reaction (rxn_016) feeds back into the network via rxn_006 (decarboxylation to acetone) or rxn_012 (homologation to α-ketoisovalerate), creating a self-reinforcing cycle.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The full cyanosulfidic pathway closely follows Patel et al. 2015 and is the most literature-grounded route in the network. The upstream hydrothermal steps (CO2→formate→formaldehyde) are plausible but the formate→formaldehyde reduction (rxn_002) is the weakest link, lacking strong experimental support under purely hydrothermal conditions. The cyanosulfidic surface steps (rxn_005 through rxn_009) are simplified representations of multi-step photoreduction cycles, but capture the essential transformations. The Strecker step (rxn_010) and hydrolysis (rxn_011) are well-established. Good Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical environmental flow.
suggestions: Specify mineral catalysts for rxn_002 (e.g., FeS or native metal surfaces)
Consider adding Cu(I)/Cu(II) sulfide catalyst specification for the cyanosulfidic surface steps (rxn_005, rxn_008, rxn_009)
Note that rxn_005 likely proceeds through dihydroxyacetone intermediate before acetone

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is critically undermined by rxn_006: thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate (CH3COCOOH) yields acetaldehyde (CH3CHO) + CO2, NOT acetone (CH3COCH3). Acetone requires a C3 + C3 condensation (e.g., via acetoacetate decarboxylation) or a completely different mechanism. This fundamental chemical error invalidates the pathway as written. Downstream steps from acetone onward are fine.
suggestions: Replace rxn_006 with a chemically correct route to acetone, e.g., via acetoacetate intermediate from two pyruvate molecules
Alternatively, replace acetone source with the cyanosulfidic glyceraldehyde route (rxn_005)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Appealingly short, and the terminal reductive amination (rxn_013) is directly supported by Kaur et al. 2024. However, rxn_012 (pyruvate + formaldehyde → α-ketoisovalerate) is a major oversimplification. Biosynthetically, this requires multiple enzymatic steps: pyruvate → acetolactate → 2,3-dihydroxyisovalerate → α-ketoisovalerate. As a single prebiotic reaction, it lacks experimental support. The C3 + C1 → C5 branched product with no intermediates is chemically implausible in one step.
suggestions: Break rxn_012 into plausible sub-steps: (1) aldol condensation of two pyruvates → acetolactate, (2) rearrangement/reduction to α-ketoisovalerate
Cite specific mineral catalysts for the homologation on Ni/Fe minerals
Consider alternative routes to α-ketoisovalerate via glyoxylate reductive homologation

pathway_evaluations.feedback: An elegant short pathway. The Strecker and hydrolysis steps are well-supported. However, rxn_014 (Fischer-Tropsch synthesis of isobutyraldehyde from CO + H2) is problematic: FTT synthesis on metal sulfides typically favors linear products (methanol, acetate, formate, linear alkanes/alcohols), and branched C4 aldehydes are not favored products. The literature notes that 'Fischer-Tropsch synthesis on metal sulfides tends to give linear acids rather than branched amino acids.' Specificity for isobutyraldehyde is not experimentally demonstrated.
suggestions: Provide specific experimental evidence for branched aldehyde formation via FTT
Specify catalyst (e.g., Fe3S4 or Fe-Ni alloy) and conditions
Consider this as a minor/speculative route rather than a primary pathway

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Biologically elegant transamination pathway, with rxn_015 (reductive amination to alanine) and rxn_016 (transamination) both well-precedented. However, the pathway inherits the critical weakness of rxn_012 (pyruvate + formaldehyde → α-ketoisovalerate). Transamination (rxn_016) requires pyridoxal-type cofactors or metal ion catalysis prebiotically, which should be specified. The regeneration of pyruvate in rxn_016 is a nice autocatalytic feature.
suggestions: Fix rxn_012 as noted in P3
Specify metal ion or pyridoxal-like catalyst for transamination step
Note that prebiotic transamination has been shown with glyoxylate and metal salts

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Starting from formaldehyde (assumed available), this surface-only cyanosulfidic pathway is well-supported by Patel et al. 2015. The formose step (rxn_004) to glyceraldehyde is classically known (requires borate or Ca(OH)2 minerals for selectivity). Subsequent cyanosulfidic steps follow the literature cascade. Avoids the weaker hydrothermal upstream steps. Environmental progression is mostly surface with final biochemical hydrolysis.
suggestions: Specify borate minerals for formose selectivity in rxn_004
Specify Cu(I)/Cu(II) sulfide catalysts for photoreduction steps

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The convergent concept is excellent—two independent routes to acetone merging at rxn_007. However, the pyruvate decarboxylation arm (rxn_006) is chemically incorrect (pyruvate gives acetaldehyde, not acetone), so only the cyanosulfidic arm (via rxn_005) actually contributes valid acetone. This removes the convergence benefit.
suggestions: Fix rxn_006 chemistry to properly produce acetone (e.g., via acetoacetate decarboxylation)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good convergence concept with two routes to isobutyraldehyde (cyanosulfidic photoreduction and Fischer-Tropsch). The cyanosulfidic arm is stronger; the FTT arm (rxn_014) is weakly supported for branched products. The Strecker + hydrolysis conclusion is solid.
suggestions: Strengthen the FTT arm with specific conditions and catalysts, or downgrade it to speculative

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Hybrid pathway that would convert α-ketoisovalerate to isobutyraldehyde for Strecker synthesis. The concept is interesting but rxn_012 remains the bottleneck. Also unclear how α-ketoisovalerate is converted to isobutyraldehyde—this reduction/decarboxylation step seems to be missing from the explicit reactions.
suggestions: Add explicit reaction for α-ketoisovalerate → isobutyraldehyde (oxidative decarboxylation)
Fix rxn_012 as discussed

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Ambitious autocatalytic cycle concept linking cyanosulfidic valine production with transamination recycling. While conceptually appealing for proto-metabolic networks, it inherits weaknesses from rxn_012. The cycle is also very long (10 steps), reducing overall yield dramatically. The autocatalytic claim requires that valine (or pyruvate) from the cycle accelerates its own production, which is plausible for the transamination loop but not clearly demonstrated.
suggestions: Clarify the autocatalytic mechanism
Fix the two problematic reactions to make the cycle viable

evaluation.network_feedback:
This is an impressively comprehensive synthesis network that integrates four literature-validated prebiotic routes to valine. The network topology is excellent with 18 molecules, 16 reactions, 5 hub molecules, and 5 convergence points. Literature alignment is strong: the Patel et al. cyanosulfidic pathway, Kaur et al. reductive amination, Miller-Schlesinger Strecker synthesis, and Johnson et al. spark-discharge chemistry are all properly represented and cited. The environmental flow (Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical) is well-structured.

However, three reactions have significant chemical issues: (1) rxn_006 (pyruvate→acetone) is incorrect—pyruvate decarboxylation yields acetaldehyde, not acetone; (2) rxn_012 (pyruvate + formaldehyde → α-ketoisovalerate) is a massive oversimplification of a multi-step biosynthetic pathway with no prebiotic precedent as a single reaction; (3) rxn_014 (Fischer-Tropsch → isobutyraldehyde) overstates FTT selectivity for branched products, which the literature itself acknowledges is unlikely. These errors affect 6 of 10 pathways.

Despite these issues, pathways P1 and P6 (the cyanosulfidic routes) remain chemically sound and well-supported, achieving valine through literature-validated Patel et al. chemistry. The network successfully reaches the target through multiple valid routes. Mineral catalysts should be more explicitly specified for surface reactions (Cu sulfides for photoreduction, borate for formose). Overall, this is a strong network that would benefit from fixing the three problematic reactions.
